text.title: Tier Structure and Psychological Pricing
text.summary: How to design tier structures (feature allocation, anchor tier), apply psychological pricing tactics, and read signals to raise or lower price.

rule.statement: Design a maximum of 3–4 tiers. Label them by target persona outcome (Starter, Pro, Team), not by technical features (Basic, Advanced, Enterprise).
rule.rationale: More than 4 options causes decision paralysis and lowers conversion. Persona labels help buyers self-select; feature labels force them to compare specs.

rule.statement: Make the middle tier the obvious anchor: give it the name "Pro" or "Growth", badge it "Most Popular", and price it at your target revenue mix. Free/Basic should be limited enough to create genuine upgrade motivation.
rule.rationale: Decoy pricing — the presence of a higher-priced option makes the middle look reasonable. "Most Popular" reduces social risk ("others have chosen this"). Free tier that gives too much removes the upgrade incentive.

list.item: Charm pricing ($49 vs $50): left-digit effect makes the number feel significantly lower. Use for sub-$200 price points.
Round pricing ($100, $500): signals premium quality. Use for high-end enterprise or luxury positioning.
Anchor high: show the most expensive tier first (left to right), so middle and lower tiers feel affordable.
Annual framing: present as "2 months free" rather than a percentage discount — tangible months feel more concrete than abstract percentages.

rule.statement: Raise price when: conversion rate exceeds 5%, customers volunteer "this is cheap", no price objections appear in sales calls, or churn is below 2%/month.
rule.rationale: High conversion at current price means willingness-to-pay is higher. You are leaving revenue on the table. Raise in 20–30% increments and re-measure.

rule.statement: Lower price (or restructure tiers) when: conversion rate is below 1%, price objections appear in more than 30% of sales calls, or sales cycles are unusually long.
rule.rationale: Below 1% conversion signals that price is above willingness-to-pay for this audience. Investigate whether the barrier is price or unclear value before lowering.

antipattern.description: Never changing prices after initial launch.
antipattern.reason: Market evolves. Costs change. Competitors adjust. Quarterly price review prevents gradual value misalignment.

antipattern.description: Hiding prices behind "contact us" for sub-$1K ARR products.
antipattern.reason: Friction and distrust. Buyers expect transparent pricing for self-serve products. "Contact us" signals the price will be painful or negotiated, which repels price-sensitive early adopters.
